items.q: How does 'der Mann' change in the accusative case?
items.why: Students think English-style: objects are unchanged. German changes ARTICLES based on case. Masculine is the only gender that visibly changes in accusative: der to den. Feminine, neuter, and plural look the same.
Students assume gender follows meaning. German gender is grammatical, not biological. The diminutive suffix -CHEN makes any noun neuter, regardless of natural gender. So das Mädchen, das Mädel.

items.q: How do you say 'I get up early' using 'aufstehen'?
items.why: Students keep separable verbs together. In present and simple past, the prefix SEPARATES and goes to the end: aufstehen becomes 'stehe...auf'. The prefix only rejoins in subordinate clauses, infinitives, and past participles.

items.q: How do you say 25 in German?
items.why: Correct answer: fünfundzwanzig. “zwanzigfünf” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Dark green. “Light green” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 35. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
